title: Number Partitioning (5 numbers)
summary: Split 5 numbers into two groups so the absolute difference of the group sums is as small as possible. 5 qubits.
problem_statement: I have a set of 5 positive numbers: [4, 3, 2, 3, 1]. Split them into two groups so the absolute difference between the two group sums is as small as possible. Tell me which numbers go in each group.
real_world_example: Balancing two teams so their total experience points are as equal as possible. Or splitting a stack of tasks between two workers so their finish times are as close as possible.

expected_pattern.variables: s_i in {-1, +1} — which group number i belongs to (equivalently x_i in {0, 1})
expected_pattern.objective: minimize (sum a_i * s_i)^2 — the squared imbalance is the entire objective

expected_solution_summary: Group A = {4, 2} (sum 6), Group B = {3, 3, 1} (sum 7). Absolute difference = 1. (Any split with difference 1 is optimal.)
learning_notes: Sum of numbers is 13 — an odd total means the minimum possible imbalance is 1, not 0. This is a useful teaching moment: the theoretically minimum objective is dictated by the input's parity. The unconstrained squared-imbalance form lowers to exactly 5 qubits with no slack.
